This is first principles thinking. If you go to a family office and you are in year zero of your startup, you are not incorporated yet, you have a, you know, a demo, a product, they're not going to invest unless they have a history of investing in entrepreneurs who are not yet incorporated and who don't have a product in market yet. Now you have to divorce what people say in social settings, especially investors, from their behavior. If you look at my firm, Founder University is designed as a product occurring in three different geographies, three different continents around the world, twice a year each, six cohorts a year: Riyadh, Tokyo, and in the US. You have now somebody who likes you, okay great, but they say that to everybody. What you want to look at is, do they actually invest in your stage of startup? The answer is probably no. Therefore, who does invest? Y Combinator, Techstars, Antler, a16z, Pear has a great summer program, Sequoia has Arc, I have Founder University launch accelerator. Go to those programs first. Get your 125k, your 25k, your 250k check, get your first five customers, and then you can go back to them and they might be a great later stage. full_transcript.text: Yeah, I mean, the number one thing is the amount of capital it takes to get a product to market and to the amount of time it takes to get product market fit. Um, that is dramatically decreased. So over time, we with vibe coding or the no code revolution, we had these moments where it seemed like really no code was one, cloud the cloud era was another, where the cost of running a startup went down 10X. Used to be 3 million, then it went to 300. And when that happened, because of WeWork, outsourced accounting and legal, and not having to stand up your own data center, it used to take 20, 30 people and 3 to 5 million dollars to get your product to market. That was in the web 1.0 era, the 90s. Seed round would be 3 million, it would take 12 months to get your product ready to go to market, and you had all of these expenses which were considered non-negotiable and you had to do them. You had to have a PR firm, 10k a month for 18 months, 180k. You had to get an office for 25k a month, you had to put a 250k letter of credit down. So that was another 300,000 a year for two years, 600,000, 250k letter of credit. It was basically a million dollars to set up your office. You had to put a half million into servers, now you're at 1.5 million. You had to spend 100k on legal, 1.6 million. You had to hire an HR person, an accountant, um, and a lawyer, another 250k, now you're at 1.8, 1.9 million, you get the idea. full_transcript.text: Oh, you went to Greece as well? Okay. I went to Santorini and Mykonos. I visited Santorini and Mykonos.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: It was amazing.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So give me your general impression of Greece and Italy. What were your favorite parts of it? What was extraordinary? And then what was underwhelming and didn't hit the notes? As we go off-duty here, this is Lon off-duty in Italy.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, so I went, I did a week in Rome. Me and a friend, we stayed in an Airbnb right by the Castel Sant'Angelo, right sort of the middle of the city, right? Not far from Trastevere. Staying in the Airbnb was amazing. We're walking distance from the Vatican. It's a building from the 1600s where the Pope's staff used to live in, like, the Renaissance era. So it was an unbelievable place. The best thing we did was that crypt tour. We did a golf cart crypt tour and a guy took us to all sorts of, like, out of the way, you know, sort of like churches that had, you know, bone temples in the basement and all of this crazy art. That was amazing to see. We took a bullet train one day to Florence and to the Uffizi Gallery. We went to this amazing pasta restaurant called Osteria Pastella that was—

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I think I went to both of those. That's great.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, yeah. They're one of the places they take a huge wheel of cheese and they pour brandy in it and then they light it on fire and then they mix your pasta in the cheese bowl and then they topped it with white truffles. It was incredible. And the Uffizi, you know, lots of great art. I will say a down thing: it is very, very crowded right now in Italy. Lots and lots of tourists want to see Rome and Florence. I was not alone. And one thing that really bugged me in all the art museums that we went to is people are standing in front of the paintings or the sculptures and they're just snapping photos on their phone. And I mean, this is like Botticelli's Birth of Venus, Caravaggio's Medusa. Just go on Google images, there's a hundred beautiful, up-close, high-resolution photos of every—

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: You can get the Birth of Venus on like a mousepad. Like, you don't need your own photo.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I think this is a- this is a gripe, this is a gripe that's worth noting.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Just be in the moment, folks, just be in the moment.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And this- I'll tell you the thing that makes me crazy in these places: The selfie stick.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: That, yes.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Or the- or the i360 guy. You know the i360 guy?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Of course. There- they're all-

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: With the stick, and it goes up, and he's got to walk around, and he's got to have a 360 view...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: ...that he's never going to look at. But the- the 360 guy is worse than the selfie guy.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The selfie guy's got his iPhone on the stick, whatever. These guys are walking through Vegas casinos and I'm like, what are you documenting? Your life is incredibly boring. You're the least important person within 100 miles of this place. This place has been documented, as you correctly point out, to the point of absurdity, and now everybody's gotta be on your i360 and you're smacking people in the head.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Nobody cares.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: You're never looking at these things. If you want to take a quick photo of yourself in front of your favorite piece, totally fine.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Right, and I did. I posted a few of them to Twitter. I would take a picture of myself, like 'here I am, I'm in the Uffizi, I want to remember this moment.' I'm not saying don't be a tourist, don't take a photo, don't take a selfie. But it's that stand- just- just take it in.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Just take it in!

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: And like we went to a Rothko exhibit as well in Florence, like a modern museum, and it's like those paintings, it's being in the room with it, that's the point. It's overwhelming when you're standing in front of it. You're not going to appreciate it on the- your phone. What- what are you doing?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So that- here's my idea. Here's my idea for these museums: 24 hours. And increase the price to go a little bit.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Mm.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: So, or maybe like on the off hours, you pay a premium.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Oh, sure.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I would like- because these all close at 6 o'clock, right? They're done.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it's like- Uffizi I think is yeah, it's like 10 AM to like 4:30, but you have to get a time. They send you through in shifts. So when you buy your tickets, they give you a time to show up and then you get in the line.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And there's like a VIP line, you pay an extra couple of euros. So what did you pay, 10 euros or something? It's not expensive.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: No, it was- I think it was like 30 euros a piece, but we also made those plans a little last minute. I bet if we got them earlier we probably could have gotten a cheaper Uffizi pass.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Here's my idea.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: The place closes- the last entrance is 4:30, which means they're kicking you out at 6:00. Right. 6:00 to 8:00, 8:00 to 10:00, 10:00 to 12:00, 12:00 to 2:00, 6:00 AM to 8:00 AM, I know maybe people don't want to work those. Have a 100 euro experience.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Now, 100 euro, 100 dollar experience per person sounds insane. But by the way, if you're an Uber driver or you're- you're a housekeeper in New York, you're making 30, 40, 50 bucks an hour already.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: And that person might want to have an experience with 10% as many people there or 20% as many people there.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, it would be a totally different experience to go with a small group.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: ...really like soak it in on your own and have the time instead of being pushed around and everybody's trying to push in front of you.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I would have loved, loved to go to dinner and then I like to do my after-dinner walk, because I'm old and, you know, controlling my weight, ro.co/twist. I would love to do my two-hour walk in the Louvre from, you know, 8:00 to 10:00 p.m. or even 10:00 to midnight. How great would it be for us to be in the Louvre at 11:00 p.m. and see the Mona Lisa or the Venus de Milo with but a half dozen people there.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah, because also I had the same experience in the British Museum too in London where it's like, it's great, amazing to see that stuff but you're, you're constantly in like this huge crowd of everybody wants to take their photos and it's really...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Did you go to the Florence gelato place that I told you about?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: You know, I did. I went to a gelato place in Florence but we were only there for 24 hours. I ran out of time. I did not get to... I missed that. I missed that one. But I did, we did get some incredible food the whole trip, Rome too, we went to some really amazing restaurants. And then, so that was week one. And then week two, we joined my dad and my uncle and his girlfriend Karen for a cruise. And that went to Santorini, Greece, Mykonos, Greece, and also a town named Kusadasi in Turkey where there's the ancient Greek ruins of Ephesus are there, and so we did a little quick tour there.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Santorini, beautiful, right? On that hillside with the... Did you get to go on the donkey ride up and down Santorini?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: No, I felt bad for the donkey. I don't want to subject a donkey to my 200 pounds. I felt like I'm not going to do that to that poor... well...

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Well, this is why ro.co/twist will get you down to 180 and you'll be fighting weight, you go on there. No, I thought the donkey thing was a little bit extreme too, but they have like this very steep hill...

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yes, it leads up to Oia. Oia, the sort of... that it's the city when you think of Santorini and you see the Instagram posts with the white houses and the blue domed churches, that's Oia. And so we got to go check out the town. We took a bus to get there. And then we went to Fira as well, another town. And then we... they took us to one of those black sand beaches so we got to do a little beach time. Santorini was incredible. That was incredible.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: I'm glad you got to take some time off. Me too. And this is where I don't have a problem with the Meta glasses actually. I'm okay having an exception for Meta glasses. If you take them to dinner, you're not invited to dinner. Right. However, if you're in a museum and you want to put those on and record, go for it. Go for it. It's better than the selfie stick everywhere.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I don't think... I still don't think you're really going to get a lot out of that after the fact. The part of being in the museum is you're there. You're in the museum. It's real. Like you can look up art on the internet all the time. I do it all the time.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: Take 10 pictures. Okay? Take 10 pictures. You yourself with the front, yourself in front of your favorite piece of art that you saw, okay. At the cafe having a coffee, whatever, having a coffee.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: I took a ton, but like, it- when you're standing in front of a Rothko, you don't need to have your camera. It's just- it's just lines on a canvas on a photo. You have to stand there and soak it in.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What are we doing folks?

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: What are we doing?

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Yeah.

full_transcript.speaker: Jason Calacanis
full_transcript.text: All right everybody, it's been another great. Twist is in the can. Bye-bye.

full_transcript.speaker: Lon Harris
full_transcript.text: Bye-bye.

highlights: To secure a seed round in the current market, founders must treat fundraising as a high-volume sales funnel, typically requiring outreach to 150 investors to yield just two term sheets.

Startups can build a moat against large language models by focusing on niche feature sets, community-driven "multiplayer modes," and specialized support services that major frontier labs are unlikely to prioritize.

The capital and time required to reach product-market fit has plummeted from millions of dollars and a year of development to just thousands of dollars and a few weeks thanks to "vibe coding" and no-code tools.

Venture capital sentiment toward hardware has shifted significantly, with investors now viewing physical products as one of the few remaining ways to achieve true customer lock-in and a defensible moat.

Success in the tech industry often involves a "sales funnel" for networking; founders should aim to meet with portfolio founders of their target VCs to build the relationships necessary for a warm introduction.
summary:
This transcript features Jason Calacanis and Lon Harris answering community questions regarding the evolving startup landscape, fundraising strategies, and industry reputations. Calacanis emphasizes that early-stage fundraising has become a rigorous sales funnel, advising founders to focus on investor behavior rather than just their words. He notes that the cost and time required to reach product-market fit have plummeted due to "vibe coding" and AI, but warns that startups must differentiate themselves from giants like OpenAI by building specialized vertical features, community-driven interfaces, and "multiplayer" modes that generic large language models are unlikely to prioritize.

Beyond tactical advice, the discussion explores the personal and ethical dimensions of Silicon Valley. Calacanis offers candid critiques of Mark Zuckerberg and Sam Altman, arguing that their perceived ruthlessness and self-interest have damaged the tech industry's reputation, despite their massive commercial success. He contrasts this with the traditional "pay-it-forward" culture of the Bay Area, which he views as a unique strength of the ecosystem. The episode concludes with a shift toward hardware startups—which Calacanis now views as a primary competitive moat—and a broader commentary on modern culture, specifically urging people to prioritize presence and "living in the moment" over documenting life for social media.
